modules.title: Refrigerant: The Foundation
modules.subtitle: What refrigerant is, why state matters, and why vapor vs. liquid shows up across Core, Type I, Type II, and Type III.

sections.heading: Big Idea
sections.body: Refrigerant moves heat by changing state. It absorbs heat when it boils into vapor and releases heat when it condenses back into liquid. Most EPA 608 questions are really asking whether you understand what state the refrigerant is in, where it is in the system, and how to handle it safely.

sections.heading: Liquid vs. Vapor
sections.body: The compressor should receive low-pressure vapor, not liquid. Liquid entering the compressor can cause slugging and damage internal parts. After the condenser, refrigerant is usually high-pressure liquid. After the evaporator, it should be low-pressure vapor.

sections.heading: Charging Rule
sections.body: Single-component refrigerants may often be charged as vapor or liquid depending on procedure, but many blended refrigerants should be charged as liquid to prevent fractionation. Fractionation means the blend composition changes because one component enters faster than another.

chart.title: Refrigerant Families
rows: Lower than CFC but still regulated
No ozone depletion, but often high GWP

typeComparison.point: Know refrigerant families, ozone/GWP concepts, recovery/recycle/reclaim, and vapor vs. liquid basics.
Small appliances have limited access and small charge sizes. Recovery may depend on appliance pressure or compressor condition.
High-pressure systems emphasize liquid recovery, compressor protection, and charging blends as liquid.
Low-pressure chillers may operate below atmospheric pressure, so air can leak inward and purge units matter.

imagePrompts: Diagram: compressor receives low-pressure vapor, condenser produces high-pressure liquid, expansion device drops pressure, evaporator boils refrigerant to vapor.
Wordless workshop panel: technician prevents liquid slugging by ensuring vapor enters compressor.

modules.title: EPA Rules, Certification, and Legal Handling
modules.subtitle: The compliance layer: who can handle refrigerant, what cannot be vented, and why recordkeeping matters.

sections.heading: Big Idea
sections.body: EPA 608 is mainly about preventing refrigerant emissions from stationary refrigeration and air-conditioning equipment. Certification proves that a technician understands legal recovery, safe handling, and environmental rules.

sections.heading: Certification Scope
sections.body: Core knowledge applies to everyone. Type I covers small appliances, Type II covers high-pressure and very-high-pressure appliances, Type III covers low-pressure appliances, and Universal covers all three types.

sections.heading: Venting Rule
sections.body: A technician should not intentionally release regulated refrigerant. Small unavoidable releases may occur during proper connection or recovery, but intentionally cutting or opening a charged system before recovery violates the basic rule.

chart.title: Certification Types
rows: Purge units, air leaks, low pressure

typeComparison.point: Legal handling, venting prohibition, certification purpose, and environmental basics.
Small appliance recovery before disposal and proper access fittings.
High-pressure recovery, repair verification, and leak thresholds.
Low-pressure evacuation, purge records, and leak management.

imagePrompts: Flowchart: Core leads to Type I, Type II, Type III, and Universal certification.
Wordless workshop panel: technician recovers refrigerant before cutting a line.

modules.title: Environment: Ozone, GWP, and Refrigerant Families
modules.subtitle: How refrigerant chemistry connects to ozone depletion, global warming potential, and phaseout rules.

sections.heading: Ozone Depletion
sections.body: CFCs and HCFCs contain chlorine. Chlorine can damage the stratospheric ozone layer. That is why older refrigerants such as CFCs and HCFCs have been phased out or heavily restricted.

sections.heading: Global Warming Potential
sections.body: GWP compares how much heat a gas traps compared with carbon dioxide. CO2 is the baseline and has a GWP of 1. HFCs do not damage ozone, but many have high GWP.

sections.heading: Low-GWP Direction
sections.body: Modern refrigerant trends are moving toward lower-GWP options such as HFOs and some natural refrigerants. These may have different flammability or handling concerns, so safety classification matters.

chart.title: Environmental Memory Chart
rows: Lower ODP than CFC but still regulated

typeComparison.point: Most environmental chemistry questions live in Core.
Small appliances may use newer low-GWP refrigerants; safe recovery still applies.
High-pressure systems commonly involve R-22, R-410A, and blend/retrofit issues.
Low-pressure chillers may use refrigerants with special pressure and purge considerations.

imagePrompts: Chart: chlorine-containing refrigerants move toward ozone depletion risk.
Visual: CO2 baseline compared to high-GWP refrigerant gas.

modules.title: Recovery: Same Rule, Different Equipment
modules.subtitle: All types require recovery, but the strategy changes with appliance size, pressure, and system design.

sections.heading: Big Idea
sections.body: Recovery means removing refrigerant from an appliance and storing it externally. The rule is shared across all EPA 608 types: do not vent refrigerant. The method changes depending on equipment type.

sections.heading: Recover, Recycle, Reclaim
sections.body: Recover means remove. Recycle means clean for reuse by filtering and oil separation. Reclaim means restore to new-product specifications, usually with chemical analysis.

sections.heading: Liquid First vs. Vapor First
sections.body: On many high-pressure systems, liquid recovery is faster because liquid carries more refrigerant mass. Vapor recovery follows after liquid is removed. On small appliances and low-pressure chillers, the recovery method depends more heavily on access, pressure, and system condition.

chart.title: Recovery by Certification Type
rows: Limited access and small charge size
Raise pressure / recover liquid and vapor
Low pressure can make recovery slower and air leaks more important

typeComparison.point: Never vent. Use proper recovery equipment and cylinders.
Know small appliance limits, passive recovery, and non-operating compressor recovery.
Know liquid recovery, major repairs, evacuation levels, and pressure testing safety.
Know purge units, low-pressure recovery, and pressure-rise checks.

imagePrompts: Diagram: recovery machine connected to appliance and recovery cylinder with arrows showing refrigerant flow.
Wordless workshop panel: liquid recovery first, then vapor recovery.

modules.title: Cylinders, DOT Rules, and the 80% Fill Limit
modules.subtitle: Why cylinder type, labeling, condition, and fill level matter for safety and compliance.

sections.heading: Cylinder Safety
sections.body: Recovery cylinders must be approved, labeled, in good condition, and not overfilled. Refrigerant expands when warmed, so a full cylinder can become dangerous if there is no expansion space.

sections.heading: 80% Rule
sections.body: A common exam rule is that recovery cylinders should not be filled beyond 80% by volume. This leaves room for expansion. Overfilling is a major safety hazard.

sections.heading: Disposable Cylinders
sections.body: Disposable cylinders are not recovery cylinders. Before disposal, remaining refrigerant should be recovered, the cylinder should be rendered unusable, and the metal should be recycled according to proper procedure.

chart.title: Cylinder Do / Do Not
rows: Recovery cylinders are built for that purpose

typeComparison.point: Most cylinder safety and DOT-style questions are Core knowledge.
Small appliance recovery still requires proper recovery containers.
Large refrigerant charges make cylinder management more important.
Chiller recovery may involve large quantities and service vessels.

imagePrompts: Wordless workshop panel: recovery cylinder on scale, technician stops at 80% fill.
Diagram: disposable cylinder marked not refillable and rendered unusable before recycling.

modules.title: Type I: Small Appliances and System-Dependent Recovery
modules.subtitle: The Type I world: sealed systems, small charges, access fittings, and passive recovery.

sections.heading: Small Appliance Definition
sections.body: Type I generally covers small appliances that are factory charged and contain 5 pounds or less of refrigerant. Examples include household refrigerators, small freezers, dehumidifiers, and some small self-contained equipment.

sections.heading: Access and Recovery
sections.body: Small appliances may not have standard service valves. Technicians may use temporary access fittings, but those fittings should be leak tested and should not be treated as permanent reliable repairs.

sections.heading: Operating vs. Non-Operating Compressor
sections.body: If the compressor works, it may help move refrigerant during system-dependent recovery. If it does not work, access to both high and low sides may be needed to recover trapped refrigerant.

typeComparison.point: Know recovery terms, safety, and no venting.
Focus on small charge size, passive recovery, access fittings, and disposal.
Similar recovery principles, but higher pressure and larger systems.
Very different pressure behavior; low-pressure chillers draw air inward.

imagePrompts: Small refrigerator sealed system diagram with compressor, capillary tube, evaporator, condenser.
Wordless workshop panel: technician installs temporary piercing valve, leak tests it, recovers refrigerant.

modules.title: Type II: High-Pressure Systems, Charging, and Compressor Protection
modules.subtitle: The field-service core: residential split systems, commercial refrigeration, high pressure, superheat, subcooling, and liquid recovery.

sections.heading: High-Pressure Systems
sections.body: Type II covers high-pressure and very-high-pressure appliances. This includes many residential and commercial systems. Pressure, airflow, charging, and compressor protection are major themes.

sections.heading: Liquid and Vapor Management
sections.body: Liquid recovery is often faster because liquid contains more refrigerant mass. But liquid refrigerant entering a compressor can cause slugging. The system must be handled in a way that protects the compressor.

sections.heading: Superheat and Subcooling
sections.body: Superheat helps evaluate vapor condition leaving the evaporator. Subcooling helps evaluate liquid condition leaving the condenser. A simple memory hook is: superheat = vapor, subcooling = liquid.

typeComparison.point: Know liquid vs. vapor and safety basics.
Small appliance systems are sealed and limited access.
High-pressure service emphasizes liquid recovery, pressure testing, charging, airflow, and compressor protection.
Low-pressure chillers require different pressure logic and purge concepts.

imagePrompts: Residential split system diagram showing indoor evaporator, outdoor condenser, compressor, and metering device.
Wordless workshop panel: liquid refrigerant slugging compressor with warning symbol.

modules.title: Type II: Leak Repair, Major Repairs, and Verification
modules.subtitle: How leak thresholds, major repairs, isolation, and verification show up on high-pressure appliance questions.

sections.heading: Major Repairs
sections.body: Major repairs involve opening important refrigerant-containing parts of the appliance, such as replacing an evaporator coil. Major repairs usually trigger stricter recovery and evacuation expectations.

sections.heading: Leak Verification
sections.body: After a leak repair, verification testing confirms that the repair worked. Some systems require an initial verification and a follow-up verification within a required time period.

sections.heading: Commercial Refrigeration Thresholds
sections.body: Large commercial systems have leak repair thresholds. The exam often asks what happens when the annualized leak rate exceeds a threshold and whether repairs, verification, retrofit, or retirement steps are required.

chart.title: Leak Repair Logic
rows: Is the appliance above the charge threshold?
Is the leak rate above the trigger?
Complete it within the required window

typeComparison.point: Know no venting and general leak safety.
Small appliance disposal and recovery are more emphasized than leak-rate calculations.
Leak thresholds, major repairs, verification, and high-pressure recovery are central.
Leak issues often appear through purge operation and low-pressure air infiltration.

imagePrompts: Flowchart: leak found → repair → initial verification → follow-up verification.
Wordless workshop panel: oil stain on coil connection leading to leak repair and verification.

modules.title: Type III: Low-Pressure Chillers and Purge Units
modules.subtitle: Why low-pressure systems are different: air leaks inward, purge units remove non-condensables, and pressure must sometimes be raised.

sections.heading: Low-Pressure Difference
sections.body: Low-pressure appliances may operate below atmospheric pressure. That means leaks can pull air and moisture into the system instead of pushing refrigerant out. This is a key Type III difference.

sections.heading: Purge Units
sections.body: A purge unit removes non-condensables such as air. If a purge unit runs excessively, that can suggest air is leaking into the chiller and the system should be leak checked.

sections.heading: Raising Pressure
sections.body: Charged low-pressure systems may be leak checked more effectively by warming the system to raise pressure. Adding heat can help reveal leaks without adding non-condensables.

typeComparison.point: General recovery and safety still apply.
Small sealed systems are not the same as low-pressure chillers.
High-pressure systems tend to leak outward; Type III may leak air inward.
Purge units, air infiltration, low-pressure recovery, and heating for leak checks dominate.

imagePrompts: Low-pressure chiller diagram showing evaporator, condenser, compressor, and purge unit.
Wordless workshop panel: air arrows entering low-pressure chiller leak point.

modules.title: Evacuation, Vacuum, and Pressure-Rise Tests
modules.subtitle: What evacuation does, why pressure rise matters, and how moisture and remaining refrigerant confuse the picture.

sections.heading: Purpose of Evacuation
sections.body: Evacuation removes air and moisture from the system before charging or after opening the system. Moisture can create acid and ice restrictions, so evacuation is both a performance and safety issue.

sections.heading: Pressure Rise
sections.body: After reaching a required vacuum, a technician may wait to see if the pressure rises. A rise can indicate a leak, remaining refrigerant boiling off, or moisture outgassing.

sections.heading: Triple Evacuation
sections.body: Triple evacuation uses evacuation and dry nitrogen breaks to help remove moisture. Nitrogen must be dry and regulated. Oxygen must never be used.

typeComparison.point: Know vacuum concepts and safety basics.
Small sealed systems can be easily contaminated when opened.
Evacuation levels and pressure-rise checks are common after service or major repair.
Low-pressure chillers may require special low-pressure evacuation logic and standing tests.

imagePrompts: Diagram: vacuum pump connected to system with micron gauge and nitrogen cylinder regulator.
Chart: vacuum level drops, then pressure-rise test line.

modules.title: Leaks, Contamination, and Safety
modules.subtitle: The practical field clues and safety rules that show up repeatedly on the exam.

sections.heading: Leak Clues
sections.body: Oil stains near fittings or tubing can indicate a refrigerant leak because refrigerant often carries oil with it. Small leaks are usually best found with electronic or ultrasonic leak detectors.

sections.heading: Contamination
sections.body: Moisture can form acids and ice restrictions. Air and non-condensables can reduce efficiency and raise pressures. Mixed refrigerants should be recovered separately and not reused as clean refrigerant.

sections.heading: Safety
sections.body: Refrigerant can cause frostbite and can displace oxygen in low areas. Nitrogen must be used with a regulator. Oxygen should never be used for pressure testing because it can create an explosion hazard with oil or refrigerant.

typeComparison.point: Know safety, oxygen displacement, cylinder rules, and never vent.
Small leaks and temporary access fittings matter because small sealed systems are easy to contaminate.
High pressure, compressor damage, nitrogen pressure testing, and condenser/evaporator issues matter.
Air infiltration, purge units, and moisture control are major concepts.

imagePrompts: Diagram: oil stain near flare fitting indicating possible refrigerant leak.
Wordless workshop panel: technician uses nitrogen regulator, never oxygen.

modules.title: Charging, Superheat, and Subcooling
modules.subtitle: A simple way to remember liquid, vapor, superheat, subcooling, and blend charging.

sections.heading: Charging Basics
sections.body: Charging is adding refrigerant to a system. The correct procedure depends on refrigerant type, system design, and manufacturer instructions. Blended refrigerants are commonly charged as liquid to maintain composition.

sections.heading: Superheat
sections.body: Superheat tells you about vapor after the evaporator. It helps confirm that the refrigerant has boiled off before reaching the compressor. Too little superheat can mean liquid return risk.

sections.heading: Subcooling
sections.body: Subcooling tells you about liquid after the condenser. It helps confirm that refrigerant leaving the condenser is liquid and can be used for charging diagnostics on many systems.

typeComparison.point: Know liquid vs. vapor and blend charging.
Small appliances are often sealed systems; charging may be less emphasized than recovery/disposal.
Superheat, subcooling, airflow, and high-pressure diagnostics are most relevant.
Low-pressure chiller charging has special vapor/liquid concerns under vacuum.

imagePrompts: Diagram: suction line superheat and liquid line subcooling points on split system.
Wordless workshop panel: blended refrigerant cylinder feeding liquid through proper charging method.

modules.title: Oils, Blends, and Compatibility
modules.subtitle: Why refrigerant, oil, and system components must match before recovery, retrofit, or charging.

sections.heading: Why Oil Matters
sections.body: Refrigerant and oil must be compatible so oil can return properly to the compressor. Wrong oil can cause lubrication problems, poor oil return, and compressor damage.

sections.heading: Blends and Fractionation
sections.body: Blended refrigerants contain multiple components. If charged incorrectly, the composition can shift. This is why liquid charging is emphasized for many blends.

sections.heading: No True Drop-In Assumption
sections.body: A replacement refrigerant is not automatically a true drop-in. A retrofit may require oil, metering device, pressure, capacity, and label changes.

typeComparison.point: Know blend, reclaim, and contamination basics.
Small appliances may use specific factory charge refrigerants and oils.
Retrofits, R-22 alternatives, R-410A handling, and oil compatibility are common.
Large systems require careful refrigerant and oil management due to cost and volume.

imagePrompts: Diagram: refrigerant cylinder, oil bottle, compressor label, and retrofit tag checklist.
Wordless workshop panel: technician refuses to mix unknown refrigerant with clean recovery cylinder.

modules.title: Exam Strategy: How to Read EPA 608 Questions
modules.subtitle: Pattern recognition for test wording: complete answer, safest answer, and type-specific clue words.

sections.heading: Choose the Complete Compliant Answer
sections.body: When a question lists several true safety or legal actions, the correct answer is often the one that includes all required steps. EPA 608 questions often reward full compliance rather than shortcuts.

sections.heading: Look for Type Clues
sections.body: Small appliance points toward Type I. High-pressure, residential split, or commercial refrigeration points toward Type II. Chiller, purge unit, below atmospheric pressure, or R-123 points toward Type III.

sections.heading: Watch for Dangerous Words
sections.body: Words like vent, oxygen, overfill, mix, cut charged line, or ignore leak usually point to wrong answers. Words like recover, label, isolate, regulate, verify, and leak test often point to safer answers.

typeComparison.point: Know universal rules: no venting, cylinder safety, environment, definitions.
Look for small appliance, sealed system, passive recovery, disposal.
Look for high pressure, major repair, liquid line, condenser, compressor.
Look for low pressure, chiller, purge unit, air infiltration, heating for leak check.

imagePrompts: Wordless workshop panel: student circles clue words in an EPA 608 question.
Chart: clue words mapped to Core, Type I, Type II, Type III.
